title: Colcannon
subtitle: Irish mashed potato with kale and spring onions
excerpt: Floury potatoes mashed with butter and milk, folded with wilted kale or cabbage and spring onions. An Irish side dish eaten throughout the British Isles with roast meats, sausages, and stews. Generously buttered, properly seasoned, and served hot.
sourceNotes: Colcannon is an Irish potato dish that has been eaten in Ireland since at least the 18th century. It is closely related to the Scottish kailkenny and the English bubble and squeak, all of which combine potato with cooked greens. The standard British version uses kale, spring onions, and a generous amount of butter.

content.text: The kale or cabbage should be wilted briefly in butter — just enough to soften and remove rawness. It is folded into the potato at the end rather than mashed in, so that the green is visible as distinct strands through the mash.

Boil the {{potato}} in salted water until completely tender, about 20 minutes. Drain thoroughly and return to the hot pan to steam-dry for 2 minutes. Meanwhile, melt half the {{unsalted-butter}} in a frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{kale}} and {{spring-onion}} and cook for 3 minutes until wilted. Season lightly. Mash the potatoes until smooth. Beat in the remaining {{unsalted-butter}} and the hot {{whole-milk}}. Season generously with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Fold in the wilted kale and spring onions until distributed but not completely mixed. Serve hot with a knob of extra butter melting on top.

items.cause: Over-worked after the milk was added, or waxy potatoes
items.fix: Use floury potatoes and mash minimally. Over-beating develops the starch and produces a gluey texture

items.symptom: Green colour bleeding into the mash, not distinct strands
items.cause: Kale chopped too finely or over-mixed
items.fix: Keep the kale in rough shreds rather than fine pieces. Fold rather than beat to keep the greens distinct

content.text: Colcannon is eaten across Ireland and Britain as a side with sausages, roast meats, and stews. In Ireland it is traditional at Halloween, when coins are baked inside the mash. The British version has lost the coin tradition but kept everything else.
